markets.name: United States
markets.tagline: World's largest stock market by market capitalisation
markets.description: The United States stock market is the world's largest by total market capitalisation — home to the most influential technology, healthcare, financial and consumer companies on earth. NYSE and NASDAQ together list over 6,000 companies. Our momentum tracker ranks a curated universe of the most liquid large-cap US stocks and surfaces the 10 showing the strongest upward momentum across daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly and annual timeframes.

markets.name: United Kingdom
markets.description: The London Stock Exchange is one of the world's oldest exchanges and remains Europe's most international market, with FTSE 100 constituents earning around three-quarters of their revenue overseas. The index is rich in energy, mining, banking and consumer-staples giants, giving it a very different character from the tech-heavy US market.

markets.name: Germany
markets.description: Germany is Europe's largest economy and the DAX 40 reflects its industrial DNA: automotive, engineering, chemicals, insurance and increasingly software and defence. Traded on Deutsche Börse's XETRA platform in Frankfurt, the DAX is a total-return index — a detail many beginners miss when comparing it with price-only indices like the FTSE 100.

markets.name: France
markets.tagline: Home of global luxury, aerospace and energy leaders
markets.description: Euronext Paris hosts the CAC 40, an index dominated by global champions in luxury goods (LVMH, Hermès, Kering), aerospace (Airbus, Safran), energy (TotalEnergies) and cosmetics (L'Oréal). Because its constituents sell worldwide, the CAC 40 often behaves more like a global consumer index than a purely French one.

markets.name: Australia
markets.tagline: Resources, banks and world-class healthcare names
markets.description: The ASX 200 blends the world's biggest miners (BHP, Fortescue, Rio Tinto) with a highly concentrated banking sector and standout global healthcare and technology firms like CSL, Pro Medicus and WiseTech. Australia's market is a favourite of income investors thanks to franking credits and high dividend payouts.

markets.name: Japan
markets.tagline: Asia's largest developed market, in a corporate-governance renaissance
markets.description: The Tokyo Stock Exchange is Asia's largest developed market. Japan's corporate governance reforms — pushing companies to lift returns on equity, unwind cross-shareholdings and return cash to shareholders — have reignited global investor interest. The market mixes world-leading automation and semiconductor-equipment makers with iconic consumer brands.

markets.name: Hong Kong
markets.tagline: The global gateway to Chinese equities
markets.description: HKEX is the primary venue where global investors access China's technology, financial and consumer giants — Tencent, Alibaba, Meituan, BYD and the big state banks all trade here. The Hang Seng Index is highly sensitive to Chinese policy shifts and global rate expectations, which makes momentum signals here especially dynamic.

markets.name: Singapore
markets.tagline: Southeast Asia's financial hub and REIT capital
markets.description: SGX anchors Southeast Asia's capital markets. The Straits Times Index is dominated by the three local banks (DBS, OCBC, UOB) — together roughly half the index — plus a deep bench of real-estate investment trusts that make Singapore Asia's REIT hub. A stable currency and strong governance make it a defensive favourite in the region.

markets.name: Malaysia
markets.tagline: ASEAN's banking, plantation and energy heartland
markets.description: Bursa Malaysia lists over 900 companies, with the KLCI dominated by banks (Maybank, CIMB, Public Bank), utilities (Tenaga Nasional), plantations and oil & gas services. Malaysia's data-centre construction boom and stable dividend payers have drawn renewed foreign interest to Kuala Lumpur in recent years.

markets.name: Indonesia
markets.tagline: Southeast Asia's largest economy and commodities giant
markets.description: The Indonesia Stock Exchange rides the growth of Southeast Asia's largest economy and its 270-million population. Big banks (BCA, BRI, Mandiri) sit alongside world-scale nickel, coal and copper producers — a mix that gives Jakarta both structural consumer growth and powerful commodity-driven momentum cycles.

markets.name: Thailand
markets.tagline: Tourism, energy and electronics on the Chao Phraya
markets.description: The Stock Exchange of Thailand blends energy heavyweights (PTT group), leading regional banks, hospital operators serving Asia's medical-tourism boom, and export electronics stars like Delta Electronics. Tourism recovery cycles and energy prices are the two big momentum drivers in Bangkok.

markets.name: Philippines
markets.tagline: Conglomerates and consumer growth in a young economy
markets.description: The Philippine Stock Exchange is characterised by powerful family conglomerates — SM, Ayala, JG Summit, Aboitiz — spanning banking, property, retail and utilities. With one of Asia's youngest populations and strong remittance flows, consumer-facing names dominate the momentum leaderboard in Manila.

markets.name: Vietnam
markets.tagline: One of Asia's fastest-growing frontier markets
markets.description: The Ho Chi Minh Stock Exchange sits at the centre of one of the world's most exciting growth stories: Vietnam's manufacturing boom, rapid urbanisation and an expected upgrade from frontier to emerging-market status. Banks, steel, technology outsourcing (FPT) and real estate dominate the leaderboard, with foreign-ownership limits adding a unique supply-demand dynamic.
